tests.name: Descriptive Statistics
tests.purpose: Summarises the basic features of your data — always run this first.
tests.whenToUse: Run before any other analysis. Describes your sample (mean age, gender distribution, score ranges) and helps you spot missing data or unusual values.
tests.tradMedExample: Describing 120 study participants: mean age 38.5 ± 12.3 years, 55% female, mean pain VAS score 4.2 ± 1.8.
tests.spssPath: Analyze → Descriptive Statistics → Descriptives (for means) or Frequencies (for categories)

keyOutputs.name: Mean / SD
keyOutputs.meaning: Average value and spread (for normally distributed continuous data)

keyOutputs.name: Median / IQR
keyOutputs.meaning: Middle value and spread (for Likert scales or skewed data)

keyOutputs.name: Frequency (%)
keyOutputs.meaning: How many participants fall in each category

keyOutputs.name: Min / Max
keyOutputs.meaning: Range — quickly spots data entry errors

tests.interpretationNote: For Likert-scale data, always report median and IQR, not mean and SD. Always report N for each variable.

tests.name: Shapiro–Wilk Normality Test
tests.purpose: Tests whether your data is normally distributed — tells you which type of test to use.
tests.whenToUse: Run this before choosing between parametric (T-Test, ANOVA) and non-parametric tests. Use Shapiro–Wilk for n < 50; use Kolmogorov–Smirnov for larger samples. Also inspect histograms and Q-Q plots — do not rely on the p-value alone.
tests.tradMedExample: Testing whether wellness scores of 35 Siddha therapy patients are normally distributed before deciding to use Independent T-Test or Mann–Whitney U.
tests.spssPath: Analyze → Descriptive Statistics → Explore → Plots: tick 'Normality plots with tests'

keyOutputs.name: p-value (Sig.)
keyOutputs.meaning: p > 0.05 → data is normally distributed → safe to use parametric tests. p < 0.05 → data is NOT normal → use non-parametric tests.

tests.interpretationNote: For n > 30, parametric tests are generally robust to mild non-normality due to the Central Limit Theorem. Always inspect the histogram shape alongside.

tests.name: Independent Samples T-Test
tests.purpose: Compares the mean scores of two separate, unrelated groups.
tests.whenToUse: Use when you have two independent groups (different people in each) and a continuous outcome. Example: treatment group vs. control group. Each participant belongs to only one group.
tests.dataType: Continuous (interval/ratio) outcome variable; categorical grouping variable with 2 levels
assumptions: Data is approximately normally distributed in each group
Groups are independent (different participants in each group)
Variances are roughly equal — check with Levene's Test
Recommended n ≥ 20 per group
tests.tradMedExample: Comparing mean pain VAS scores between patients receiving Siddha herbal treatment (n=40) vs. placebo group (n=40) after 4 weeks.
tests.spssPath: Analyze → Compare Means → Independent Samples T-Test

keyOutputs.name: t-statistic
keyOutputs.meaning: Size of the difference relative to data variability

keyOutputs.name: p-value (Sig. 2-tailed)
keyOutputs.meaning: p < 0.05 → the difference between groups is statistically significant

keyOutputs.name: Mean Difference
keyOutputs.meaning: The actual numerical gap between the two group averages

keyOutputs.name: 95% CI
keyOutputs.meaning: Range within which the true mean difference falls

tests.interpretationNote: Check Levene's Test first: if p > 0.05, use the 'Equal variances assumed' row; if p < 0.05, use 'Equal variances NOT assumed'. Always report effect size (Cohen's d).

tests.name: Paired Samples T-Test
tests.purpose: Compares scores from the same participants at two time points (pre vs. post).
tests.whenToUse: Use when the SAME participants are measured twice — typically before and after an intervention. Each person contributes two data points.
tests.dataType: Continuous data — same participants measured at two time points
assumptions: The SAME participants are measured at both time points
The differences between pairs are approximately normally distributed
No extreme outliers in the difference scores
tests.tradMedExample: Measuring blood glucose levels in the same 30 patients before and after 8 weeks of Ayurvedic treatment.
tests.spssPath: Analyze → Compare Means → Paired Samples T-Test

keyOutputs.name: t-statistic
keyOutputs.meaning: Magnitude of the pre–post change

keyOutputs.name: p-value (Sig. 2-tailed)
keyOutputs.meaning: p < 0.05 → there is a statistically significant change after treatment

keyOutputs.name: Mean Difference
keyOutputs.meaning: Average change from pre to post (positive = increase, negative = decrease)

tests.interpretationNote: Report the direction of change (improved or worsened) and the mean difference. Always report effect size (Cohen's d).

tests.name: One-Way ANOVA
tests.purpose: Compares mean scores across three or more independent groups simultaneously.
tests.whenToUse: Use when comparing 3+ independent groups on a continuous outcome. A significant ANOVA only tells you differences EXIST — always follow with post-hoc tests (Tukey or Bonferroni) to find WHICH groups differ.
tests.dataType: Continuous outcome variable; categorical grouping variable with 3+ levels
assumptions: Data is approximately normally distributed in each group
Groups are independent
Variances are roughly equal across groups (Levene's Test p > 0.05)
No significant outliers
tests.tradMedExample: Comparing quality-of-life scores across three groups: TCM treatment, Ayurvedic treatment, and no-treatment control.
tests.spssPath: Analyze → Compare Means → One-Way ANOVA → Post Hoc: Tukey or Bonferroni → Options: tick Descriptive + Homogeneity of variance

keyOutputs.name: F-statistic
keyOutputs.meaning: Overall test of whether any group differs

keyOutputs.name: p-value (Sig.)
keyOutputs.meaning: p < 0.05 → at least one group is significantly different from another

keyOutputs.name: Post-hoc tests (Tukey/Bonferroni)
keyOutputs.meaning: Shows WHICH specific pairs of groups differ significantly

keyOutputs.name: η² (Eta-squared)
keyOutputs.meaning: Effect size: 0.01 = small, 0.06 = medium, 0.14 = large

tests.interpretationNote: A significant F does NOT tell you where the differences are — always run post-hoc tests. Report η² as effect size.

tests.name: Mann–Whitney U Test
tests.purpose: Non-parametric alternative to the Independent T-Test for non-normal or ordinal data.
tests.whenToUse: Use when comparing two independent groups but your data is NOT normally distributed, is ordinal (Likert scales), or your sample is small. Compares medians by ranking all scores together.
tests.dataType: Ordinal or continuous data when normality assumption is not met
assumptions: Data is at least ordinal
Groups are independent
Same distribution shape in both groups (for interpreting median differences)
tests.tradMedExample: Comparing median patient-rated improvement scores between herbal treatment and placebo groups when pain scores are skewed or sample is small (n < 30).
tests.spssPath: Analyze → Nonparametric Tests → Legacy Dialogs → 2 Independent Samples → select Mann–Whitney U

keyOutputs.name: U-statistic
keyOutputs.meaning: The test statistic

keyOutputs.name: p-value (Asymp. Sig.)
keyOutputs.meaning: p < 0.05 → the two groups are significantly different

keyOutputs.name: Median
keyOutputs.meaning: Report median and IQR (not mean and SD) for non-parametric tests

tests.interpretationNote: Report as: 'Mann–Whitney U test indicated a significant difference between groups, U = X, p = .003.' Always report medians with IQR.

tests.name: Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test
tests.purpose: Non-parametric alternative to the Paired T-Test for pre–post data that is not normally distributed.
tests.whenToUse: Use when the SAME participants are measured twice but data is not normal, is ordinal, or sample is small (n < 30).
tests.dataType: Ordinal or continuous paired data when normality is not met
assumptions: Same participants measured at two time points
Data is at least ordinal
The differences between pairs are symmetrically distributed
tests.tradMedExample: Comparing symptom severity in the same 20 patients before and after Siddha detoxification therapy when sample size is small.
tests.spssPath: Analyze → Nonparametric Tests → Legacy Dialogs → 2 Related Samples → Wilcoxon

keyOutputs.name: Z-statistic
keyOutputs.meaning: The standardised test statistic

keyOutputs.name: p-value (Asymp. Sig.)
keyOutputs.meaning: p < 0.05 → significant pre–post change occurred

tests.interpretationNote: Report medians with IQR for both time points. Use this when n < 30 or normality is violated for paired data.

tests.name: Kruskal–Wallis Test
tests.purpose: Non-parametric alternative to One-Way ANOVA for comparing 3+ groups with non-normal or ordinal data.
tests.whenToUse: Use when comparing three or more independent groups and data is not normally distributed or is ordinal. Follow with pairwise Mann–Whitney U tests using Bonferroni correction.
tests.dataType: Ordinal or non-normal continuous data across 3+ independent groups
assumptions: Data is at least ordinal
Groups are independent
Each group has at least 5 participants
tests.tradMedExample: Comparing patient satisfaction Likert scores (1–5) across three TCM clinics when scores are skewed.
tests.spssPath: Analyze → Nonparametric Tests → Legacy Dialogs → K Independent Samples → Kruskal–Wallis H

keyOutputs.name: H-statistic (Chi-Square in output)
keyOutputs.meaning: The overall test statistic

keyOutputs.name: p-value (Asymp. Sig.)
keyOutputs.meaning: p < 0.05 → at least one group differs from another

tests.interpretationNote: Follow with pairwise Mann–Whitney U tests (Bonferroni-corrected) to find which groups differ. Report median and IQR per group.

tests.name: Pearson Correlation
tests.purpose: Measures the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two continuous variables.
tests.whenToUse: Use when both variables are continuous and normally distributed, and you want to know if they are related. Result (r) ranges from −1 to +1.
tests.dataType: Two continuous variables, both approximately normally distributed
assumptions: Both variables are continuous (interval or ratio scale)
Both variables are approximately normally distributed
A linear relationship exists between the variables
No extreme outliers
tests.tradMedExample: Examining the relationship between number of Ayurvedic treatment sessions attended and improvement in wellness scores.

keyOutputs.name: r (correlation coefficient)
keyOutputs.meaning: Strength and direction: +1 = perfect positive, −1 = perfect negative, 0 = no relationship. Strength guide: < 0.20 = very weak, 0.20–0.39 = weak, 0.40–0.59 = moderate, 0.60–0.79 = strong, ≥ 0.80 = very strong.

keyOutputs.name: p-value (Sig.)
keyOutputs.meaning: p < 0.05 → the relationship is statistically significant

keyOutputs.name: r² (square of r)
keyOutputs.meaning: % of shared variance. E.g., r = 0.60 → r² = 0.36 → 36% shared variance.

tests.interpretationNote: Correlation does NOT imply causation. Always report both r and p-value. Use Spearman for Likert-scale data.

tests.name: Spearman Rank Correlation
tests.purpose: Non-parametric measure of monotonic relationship between two ordinal or non-normal variables.
tests.whenToUse: Use when variables are ordinal (Likert scales), not normally distributed, or contain outliers. Very common in health and social science research with questionnaire data.
assumptions: Variables are at least ordinal
A monotonic relationship exists (as one variable increases, the other consistently increases or decreases)
tests.tradMedExample: Examining the relationship between patient-rated symptom severity (1–10) and quality-of-life scores after herbal therapy.

keyOutputs.name: ρ (rho)
keyOutputs.meaning: Strength and direction of relationship (−1 to +1) — interpreted the same way as Pearson's r

keyOutputs.name: p-value (Sig.)
keyOutputs.meaning: p < 0.05 → statistically significant relationship

tests.interpretationNote: The safer choice for any Likert-scale data. Interpretation of strength is identical to Pearson's r.

tests.name: Chi-Square Test of Independence
tests.purpose: Tests whether two categorical variables are associated or independent of each other.
tests.whenToUse: Use when BOTH variables are categorical. Example: Is there an association between treatment type (herbal/conventional) and treatment outcome (improved/not improved)?
assumptions: Both variables are categorical
Expected frequency in each cell must be ≥ 5 (if not, use Fisher's Exact Test)
Each participant is counted only once
Random or representative sampling
tests.tradMedExample: Testing whether gender (male/female) is associated with treatment preference (TCM / Ayurveda / Conventional) in 200 clinic visitors.
tests.spssPath: Analyze → Descriptive Statistics → Crosstabs → Statistics: tick Chi-Square and Phi & Cramer's V

keyOutputs.name: χ² (Chi-Square value)
keyOutputs.meaning: The test statistic

keyOutputs.name: p-value (Asymp. Sig.)
keyOutputs.meaning: p < 0.05 → a statistically significant association exists between the two variables

keyOutputs.name: Cramér's V
keyOutputs.meaning: Effect size: 0.10 = small, 0.30 = medium, 0.50 = large association

tests.interpretationNote: Always report Cramér's V as the effect size. If any expected cell count is < 5, use Fisher's Exact Test instead (available in same SPSS dialog).

tests.name: Multiple Linear Regression
tests.purpose: Predicts a continuous outcome from one or more predictor variables and identifies their relative contributions.
tests.whenToUse: Use when you want to know which factors best predict a continuous outcome, and by how much. Requires a larger sample (rule of thumb: 10–20 participants per predictor).
tests.dataType: Continuous outcome variable; continuous or dummy-coded categorical predictors
assumptions: Linear relationship between each predictor and the outcome
Residuals are approximately normally distributed
No multicollinearity (VIF < 10 for all predictors)
Homoscedasticity (equal variance of residuals across predicted values)
Recommended: 10–20 participants per predictor variable
tests.tradMedExample: Predicting patient wellness scores from number of treatment sessions, treatment type (dummy-coded), and patient age in a TCM intervention study.
tests.spssPath: Analyze → Regression → Linear → Enter method (or Stepwise for exploratory) → Statistics: tick Collinearity diagnostics

keyOutputs.name: R² (R-squared)
keyOutputs.meaning: How much variance in the outcome is explained by all predictors combined. E.g., R² = 0.42 → 42% explained.

keyOutputs.name: β (Standardised Beta)
keyOutputs.meaning: Relative contribution of each predictor — higher |β| = stronger predictor

keyOutputs.name: p-value for each predictor
keyOutputs.meaning: p < 0.05 → that predictor significantly contributes to the model

keyOutputs.name: VIF
keyOutputs.meaning: Variance Inflation Factor — VIF > 10 indicates problematic multicollinearity

tests.interpretationNote: Report Adjusted R² (more conservative than R²) when you have multiple predictors. Report β and p for each significant predictor.

tests.name: Binary Logistic Regression
tests.purpose: Predicts the probability of a binary (yes/no) outcome from one or more predictor variables.
tests.whenToUse: Use when your outcome variable has only two categories — e.g., treatment response (yes/no), disease relapse (yes/no), treatment adherence (compliant/non-compliant).
tests.dataType: Binary outcome (0/1 or yes/no); any type of predictor variables
assumptions: Outcome variable is binary (two categories only)
Observations are independent
No extreme multicollinearity among predictors
Recommended: at least 10 events per predictor variable
tests.tradMedExample: Predicting likelihood of symptom remission (yes/no) based on treatment type, dosage, and patient age in an Ayurvedic clinical study.

keyOutputs.name: Exp(B) / Odds Ratio (OR)
keyOutputs.meaning: How much the odds of the outcome change per unit increase in the predictor. OR > 1 = increased odds; OR < 1 = decreased odds.

keyOutputs.name: Nagelkerke R²
keyOutputs.meaning: Pseudo R-squared — approximation of explained variance

keyOutputs.name: Hosmer–Lemeshow p-value
keyOutputs.meaning: Should be p > 0.05 to indicate good model fit

keyOutputs.name: Classification Table
keyOutputs.meaning: Overall percentage of correct predictions

tests.interpretationNote: Always report 95% Confidence Interval for Exp(B). An OR of 1.0 means no effect; report whether the CI excludes 1.0.

tests.name: Cronbach's Alpha
tests.purpose: Measures whether questionnaire items consistently measure the same underlying construct.
tests.whenToUse: Run this for every questionnaire or scale before conducting other analyses. A prerequisite for valid results. Minimum acceptable α = 0.70 for research.
tests.dataType: Multiple Likert-scale or rating items that measure one construct
assumptions: All items measure the same single underlying construct (unidimensionality)
Items are scored in the same direction — reverse-score negatively worded items first
Items should be moderately correlated with each other
tests.tradMedExample: Testing whether 10 items measuring 'perceived wellness after TCM therapy' consistently measure that construct (target α ≥ 0.70).
tests.spssPath: Analyze → Scale → Reliability Analysis → Model: Alpha → Statistics: tick Item, Scale if item deleted

keyOutputs.name: Cronbach's Alpha (α)
keyOutputs.meaning: ≥ 0.90 = Excellent · 0.80–0.89 = Good · 0.70–0.79 = Acceptable · 0.60–0.69 = Questionable · < 0.60 = Poor (unacceptable for research)

keyOutputs.name: CITC (Corrected Item-Total Correlation)
keyOutputs.meaning: How well each item correlates with the overall scale. Target ≥ 0.30. Items < 0.20 should be reviewed for removal.

keyOutputs.name: Alpha if Item Deleted
keyOutputs.meaning: If deleting an item raises α substantially, consider removing that item.

tests.interpretationNote: Always check CITC for each item. Never report α without also checking whether items were reverse-scored where needed.

tests.name: Test-Retest Reliability (ICC)
tests.purpose: Measures whether the questionnaire produces stable, consistent results across two administrations.
tests.whenToUse: Administer the same questionnaire to the same participants twice (typically 2–4 weeks apart). Use ICC to quantify stability over time. Required for instrument validation studies.
tests.dataType: Two sets of continuous scores from the same participants
assumptions: Same participants complete the questionnaire at two time points
The interval between administrations is appropriate (typically 1–4 weeks)
The construct being measured should be stable over the test interval
tests.tradMedExample: Administering a 'perceived herbal efficacy' scale to 40 patients twice, 2 weeks apart, to verify scale stability before the main study.
tests.spssPath: Analyze → Scale → Reliability Analysis → Intraclass Correlation Coefficient → Two-Way Mixed, Absolute Agreement

keyOutputs.name: ICC value
keyOutputs.meaning: ≥ 0.90 = Excellent · 0.75–0.89 = Good · 0.50–0.74 = Moderate · < 0.50 = Poor

keyOutputs.name: 95% Confidence Interval
keyOutputs.meaning: Range within which the true ICC value falls — both bounds should be ≥ 0.70 for clinical use

tests.interpretationNote: ICC ≥ 0.75 is generally acceptable for research. For clinical measurement tools, aim for ICC ≥ 0.90.

tests.name: Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA)
tests.purpose: Discovers the underlying factor structure of a set of questionnaire items when structure is not yet known.
tests.whenToUse: Use EFA when DEVELOPING a new questionnaire or when you have no prior theory about how items group together. Use CFA when you want to CONFIRM a previously known structure.
tests.dataType: Multiple Likert-scale or continuous items (typically 10–100 items)
assumptions: Sample size ≥ 100 (minimum 5 participants per item)
Items are at least ordinal
Kaiser–Meyer–Olkin (KMO) ≥ 0.60 — tests sampling adequacy
Bartlett's Test of Sphericity: p < 0.05 — tests that items are correlated
No items with inter-item correlation > 0.90 (multicollinearity)
tests.tradMedExample: Identifying the underlying factors (physical, mental, and spiritual wellness) in a new 30-item Traditional Medicine Wellness Scale.
tests.spssPath: Analyze → Dimension Reduction → Factor → Extraction: Principal Axis Factoring → Rotation: Promax (oblique) or Varimax (orthogonal) → Suppress loadings below 0.30

keyOutputs.name: KMO value
keyOutputs.meaning: ≥ 0.80 = Good · 0.70–0.79 = Middling · 0.60–0.69 = Mediocre · < 0.60 = Unacceptable — abort EFA if < 0.60

keyOutputs.name: Bartlett's Test p-value
keyOutputs.meaning: Must be p < 0.05 to confirm items are sufficiently correlated for factor analysis

keyOutputs.name: Factor Loadings
keyOutputs.meaning: Strength of each item's relationship to each factor. Target: ≥ 0.40 (remove items < 0.30)

keyOutputs.name: Variance Explained (%)
keyOutputs.meaning: Total variance accounted for by all retained factors. Target: ≥ 50%

keyOutputs.name: Eigenvalues
keyOutputs.meaning: Values > 1.0 indicate a factor worth retaining (Kaiser criterion). Cross-check with Scree plot.

tests.interpretationNote: Remove items with loadings < 0.40 or that cross-load on two factors with < 0.10 difference. Use Scree plot + parallel analysis to determine the number of factors.

tests.name: Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA)
tests.purpose: Tests whether a predefined factor structure fits the observed data in your sample.
tests.whenToUse: Use CFA when you have a theoretically-derived or previously validated questionnaire and want to confirm its factor structure holds in your sample. Requires AMOS (SPSS add-on) or R (lavaan).
tests.dataType: Multiple Likert-scale or continuous items with a predefined factor structure
assumptions: Sample size ≥ 200 (or at least 10 participants per item)
Items are approximately normally distributed
A theoretical factor structure is pre-specified before data collection
No extreme multivariate outliers
tests.tradMedExample: Confirming that a previously validated 3-factor TCM Wellness Scale maintains its structure in a Malaysian sample.
tests.spssPath: Use IBM SPSS AMOS (add-on): Launch AMOS from SPSS, draw the factor structure path diagram, run analysis

keyOutputs.name: CFI (Comparative Fit Index)
keyOutputs.meaning: ≥ 0.95 = good fit · ≥ 0.90 = acceptable · < 0.90 = poor fit

keyOutputs.name: RMSEA
keyOutputs.meaning: ≤ 0.06 = good fit · ≤ 0.08 = acceptable · > 0.10 = poor fit

keyOutputs.name: SRMR
keyOutputs.meaning: ≤ 0.08 = good fit

keyOutputs.name: Standardised Factor Loadings
keyOutputs.meaning: Should be ≥ 0.50 for each item (ideally ≥ 0.70)

keyOutputs.name: AVE (Average Variance Extracted)
keyOutputs.meaning: ≥ 0.50 = acceptable convergent validity

keyOutputs.name: CR (Composite Reliability)
keyOutputs.meaning: ≥ 0.70 = acceptable reliability

tests.interpretationNote: Report CFI, RMSEA, and SRMR as primary fit indices. If fit is poor, inspect modification indices — but only make theoretically justifiable changes.

tests.name: Effect Size
tests.purpose: Measures the practical significance of a finding — not just WHETHER a difference exists, but HOW BIG it is.
tests.whenToUse: Always report alongside p-values. A statistically significant result can still be practically trivial (especially with large samples). Most journals now require effect sizes.
tests.dataType: Calculated from your statistical output — not a standalone test
tests.tradMedExample: Reporting that herbal treatment produced a large effect on pain reduction (Cohen's d = 0.85), not just that it was significant (p = 0.003).
tests.spssPath: T-Tests: SPSS reports Cohen's d in newer versions, or calculate: d = (M1–M2) ÷ pooled SD · ANOVA: η² = SS_between ÷ SS_total from output

keyOutputs.name: Cohen's d (T-Tests)
keyOutputs.meaning: 0.20 = small · 0.50 = medium · 0.80 = large effect

keyOutputs.name: η² / partial η² (ANOVA)
keyOutputs.meaning: 0.01 = small · 0.06 = medium · 0.14 = large effect

keyOutputs.name: r (Correlation)
keyOutputs.meaning: 0.10 = small · 0.30 = medium · 0.50 = large effect

keyOutputs.name: Cramér's V (Chi-Square)
keyOutputs.meaning: 0.10 = small · 0.30 = medium · 0.50 = large effect

tests.interpretationNote: Always report effect size. Never only report p-values. Journals like PLOS ONE, BMC, and Frontiers require effect sizes.

thresholds.text: Highly statistically significant (p < 0.001). The probability of observing this result by chance alone is less than 0.1%. There is very strong evidence against the null hypothesis.
Statistically significant (p < 0.01). Strong evidence against the null hypothesis. This result would occur by chance less than 1% of the time.
Statistically significant (p < 0.05). The conventional threshold for significance in most research. There is sufficient evidence to reject the null hypothesis.
Marginally significant (0.05 < p < 0.10). This result does not meet the conventional significance threshold. Consider it a trend — interpret with caution and do not make strong claims.
Not statistically significant (p > 0.10). There is insufficient evidence to reject the null hypothesis. This does not prove the null hypothesis is true — it may reflect insufficient power.
Excellent internal consistency reliability (α ≥ 0.90). The questionnaire items very consistently measure the same underlying construct.
Good internal consistency reliability (α = {value}). The questionnaire items consistently measure the intended construct. Acceptable for all research purposes.
Acceptable internal consistency reliability (α = {value}). The questionnaire meets the minimum standard for research (α ≥ 0.70) and can be used in the study.
Questionable internal consistency (α = {value}). This falls below the 0.70 threshold. Review items with low CITC (< 0.30) and consider revising or removing them before proceeding.
Poor internal consistency (α = {value}). The questionnaire items do not consistently measure the same construct. The scale requires substantial revision. Do not use this scale in the current form.
Very strong positive correlation (r = {value}). The two variables are very strongly related — as one increases, the other increases substantially.
Strong positive correlation (r = {value}). The two variables are strongly related. This relationship is likely practically meaningful.
Moderate positive correlation (r = {value}). A meaningful relationship exists between the variables, though other factors also play a role.
Weak positive correlation (r = {value}). A slight positive relationship exists but the association is not strong. Many other factors likely influence the outcome.
Very weak or no positive correlation (r = {value}). The two variables show little to no linear relationship.
Marvellous sampling adequacy (KMO = {value}). The data is highly suitable for factor analysis.
Good sampling adequacy (KMO = {value}). The data is well-suited for factor analysis.
Middling sampling adequacy (KMO = {value}). The data is acceptable for factor analysis.
Mediocre sampling adequacy (KMO = {value}). Factor analysis can proceed but with caution. Consider collecting more data.
Unacceptable sampling adequacy (KMO = {value}). Factor analysis should NOT be conducted with this data. The items are not sufficiently correlated.

items: Define a clear, focused research title
Write 3–5 specific research objectives
Formulate hypotheses (null and alternative) for each objective
Select an appropriate study design (cross-sectional, experimental, quasi-experimental, etc.)
Classify your variables as independent, dependent, or covariate
Determine required sample size (use G*Power — α = 0.05, power = 0.80)
Define inclusion and exclusion criteria for participants
Obtain ethics approval / institutional review board clearance
Identify the questionnaire(s) / measurement tools to be used
Check whether existing validated instruments are available before developing new ones
Pilot-test questionnaire on a small sample (n = 20–30) before main study
Run Cronbach's Alpha on pilot data (target α ≥ 0.70)
Revise items with CITC < 0.30 based on pilot results
Collect data from the target sample using standardised procedure
Record how and when data was collected (for methodology section)
Ensure data entry accuracy — double-check a random sample of records
Back up your data file immediately after collection
Check for missing data — acceptable threshold: < 5% per variable
Handle missing values (listwise deletion, mean imputation, or multiple imputation)
Identify and investigate outliers (use boxplots and z-scores > ±3)
Reverse-score all negatively worded questionnaire items
Check variable types and coding in SPSS (nominal, ordinal, scale)
Run descriptive statistics first to verify data makes sense
Test normality (Shapiro–Wilk for n < 50; Kolmogorov–Smirnov for larger samples)
Run and report descriptive statistics (means, SD, frequencies, percentages)
Test all statistical assumptions before running main analyses
Choose parametric tests if data is normal; non-parametric if not
Run reliability analysis (Cronbach's Alpha) for all questionnaire scales
Run all main statistical tests as specified in your research objectives
Calculate and report effect sizes (Cohen's d, η², r, Cramér's V)
Create clear, properly labelled tables for all key results
Verify all reported values match SPSS output exactly
Write Abstract: background, objective, method, results, conclusion (150–250 words)
Write Introduction: background, research gap, significance, objectives
Write Methodology: design, participants, instruments, procedure, analysis plan
Write Results: present all findings with tables and figures (no interpretation yet)
Write Discussion: interpret findings, compare with literature, explain unexpected results
Write Limitations and Conclusion
Format all references in APA 7th edition style
Review target journal's author guidelines and word limit
Check for plagiarism (target: < 15% similarity for most journals)
Have a colleague or mentor review the manuscript before submission
